223276056.review: this is one of those games that sticks with you long after you have finished playing; not because it depends on shock value, but because of how emotionally heavy the story is. At first, this indie game seems simple in style, but as you play, it becomes clear that the plot is disturbing and makes you think. It mixes psychological horror with concepts that feel real and honest instead of over the top. The game does a great job of forming an eerie and contemplative atmosphere that makes its characters and core concepts feel like they were meant to be there and are personal to the player. It might not stand out for how complicated the mechanics are, but that does not matter as much as how well it tells a story. It does not talk too much about itself, which shows respect for the player and allows room for interpretation. At the end, it's evident that Sally Face is not so much about how you play the game as it is about the feeling it leaves behind, which is impossible to copy and much harder to forget

222736714.review:
Sally Face is well worth your time especially if you are into story heavy, indie, and/or creepy games. the story and characters keep you hooked the whole time and you'll find yourself unable to put the game down. 

I think the game could've been a bit longer. the initial charm of the slow and mysterious pacing in the first 3 chapters were lost to the timeskip and the over-explanation of the in-between happenings. It felt a bit ungratifying to have such big mysteries revealed in ways that felt more like "telling" than "showing" on top of having been left out of the experience completely. maybe the pacing could've been more consistent if there were more episodes in between. however, I am aware that my opinions may also be influenced by the fact that I experienced playing the whole game straight and not episodically upon release. 

overall, Sally Face is still a great story and game and I am filled with admiration for the fact that this was a solo dev project. I thoroughly enjoyed all the experimentation with visual styles and gameplay especially in the last episode, and I very much look forward to playing Sally Face 2 someday.

220426212.review:
Sally Face is one of those rare indie gems that stays with you long after you finish it. The game’s dark atmosphere is its strongest suit—every chapter feels like peeling back layers of a disturbing yet strangely heartfelt story. 
The hand-drawn art style fits perfectly.
Gameplay-wise, it’s a mix of exploration, puzzle-solving, and narrative-driven choices. While the mechanics are simple, they serve the story rather than distract from it. The pacing can feel slow at times, but that deliberate tempo builds tension and makes the emotional moments hit harder.
What really sets Sally Face apart is its storytelling. It tackles heavy themes— fear, loss, trauma, friendship, and the supernatural.
After spending a long time with this game, I can confidently say it’s not just entertainment—it’s an experience. If you enjoy dark, atmospheric adventures with a strong narrative, Sally Face is absolutely worth your time.

217721937.review:
'Sally Face' is a truly legendary game.

Like many here, I played it for the first time when I was a teen, and it left a strong impression on me. 
Even though many years have passed, the moment I start the first chapter, I am immediately drawn into this deep mystery and emotional rollercoaster all over again.

SF is notably nostalgic. Every part of this game exudes an old-school atmosphere. The cartoonesque art style, filled with many easter eggs, is a great example of it. If you were born anywhere between the 80s and the early 00s, the game will evoke a strong sense of belonging due to its world-building and aesthetic. 
The gruesomeness and 'dirtiness' of the plot and visuals, coupled with the purity and wholesomeness of the main characters and their relationships, create this deep connection to the game, make you really immersed in it.

The writing itself is elaborate and of a good quality. Every chapter is thoughtfully paced and delivered. Except, I would say, the ending. I just wish we got more from it, but alas, we gotta enjoy what we have.

Overall, I believe it's a must-play for anyone who can relate to the vibe of the game, anyone who was into conspiracy theories at least once in their life, and practically anyone who likes a good story-based horror game.

216371523.review:
Sally Face is a game with a strangely remarkable narrative and creative visuals. It is an original game, made with great care. It is evident that Steve Gabry (its creator) dedicated a lot of effort to every detail, especially in delivering the narrative, which contains an intriguing emotional depth. 
The strongest aspect of this game is its narrative; therefore, the player should definitely play it patiently, savoring the beautiful melancholy of the music and the multiple stories being told. At several moments, the game surprises the player with tragic details, some requiring more attention than others, as they intertwine with stories not directly told in the narrative. Unexpected events significantly change the perception of the characters, especially the protagonist. Many times I found myself attached to characters who had to follow paths of no return, even though I wished I could undo their tragedies. The urge I felt was to try to ease their pain, to prevent the inevitable, but the game itself subtly shows that everything happens for a reason. Bad things can be medicine for the soul, even if many suffer because of them. This is a beautiful game about suffering: about trauma, about how cosmic conflicts relate to the private sorrows within each person’s mind. 
The art style was very well used, blending childishness with psychological horror. I believe that even though the style is a beautifully strange cartoon, the childish factor has the greater purpose of affirming just how truly strange childhood is. Friends come and go, mistakes are made, but in some mysterious way everything done during that time marks us for life. The horrors experienced by the characters are exposed in their own conceptual art, which is also why each character is iconic, with shape, color, and concept describing from the inside out who they are. Sal is a character who reveals more about himself through his mask than without it. 
The weak point also lies in the narrative. The dialogues could have delved deeper into the motivations, feelings, and purposes of each character. I felt that the game has a rich world, with characters who have fascinating backstories, but few opportunities to reveal those gems. At the end of the game, I felt that the narrative sped up more than it should have, though nothing that ruined the experience. It’s just that the ending should have explored better the conclusion of the events and each character involved. I feel that if the game’s script had allowed itself more time to give more space to certain characters and certain historical conflicts that affect the narrative in parallel, this game would have been far more effective. Many times I was moved by events that gave no time to breathe, as they were too rushed. The cutscenes, the music, and the texts need to give more space for the player to feel their own feelings. 
For those who enjoy Silent Hill, Cry of Fear, Afraid of Monsters, Max Payne, and many other games that explore the depths of the human mind, this game is yet another example that horror games are the most beautiful ones that exist. Congratulations, Steve Gabry!

This game really isn't nearly as deep or gripping as people say. I think the only thing this game really has going for it is the art direction. Considering how many people compare this to Omori (but better!), my expectations were set high... Only to find the story extremely shallow and the gameplay extremely tedious and boring.

This game was honestly a chore to play. I found myself just spamming the Enter key while holding one of the arrow keys. This is a puzzle game, but a lot of the puzzles lack context, and don't feel very satisfying to complete, There's two or three little "fights" but they're basically just QTEs.

The story isn't bad enough to be on par with a creepypasta, but it's not much better. I found Omori's story much more gripping, mature, and sadly relatable; unlike Sally Face. The whole prosthetic mask thing was pretty stupid and basically just felt like Jeff the Killer; which isn't far off. It also became extremely convoluted with this almighty Eldritch cult. I'm actually struggling to recall much of the story because of how uninteresting it was, whereas I can easily recall plenty of moments from Omori.

The one place this game really shines is the art styles and the soundtrack. I loved seeing the different realities in the late game. Basically the game just explores a bunch of different and very fun art styles; the only issue is you don't need to buy the game to enjoy that.

Overall, this game is bad and I have no intention of playing it again. Apparently it's the developer's first game, so hopefully the dev can make better projects in the future I feel like they're better with art design than story/gameplay. 3/10 Don't bother playing it, even on sale.

213373688.review: Sally Face is a game that has changed my life forever. I played this game well after all the episodes were released and only really heard about it when watching old Jacksepticeye videos. I watched maybe 5 to 10 minutes of gameplay and immediately went over to steam to purchase the first episode. I played through and fell in love within the first 20 minutes of the game. Larry and Sal's friendship stood out to me, so of course, I fell for the metalheads. I struggled a lot with mental health and SH in high school and I still do to this day. Episode after episode I just couldn't stop playing, and I began to listen to the soundtrack day and night and even all day at school. Eventually, I started drawing and sketching sally face doodles in class to help distract myself from being in my head all the time, and even bonded with my friend over my hyperfixation of the game. I was very alone when i was a teen and playing sally face while i sat in my room all day always cheered me up and led me to interact with people on art forms. I cried, smiled, and laughed throughout my time playing it, and it was one of my happiest moments in the darkness that was my life at the time. Eventually, I made it to the end of the game, and I was moved by it. It changed my outlook on life, helped me through my darkest days, and even saved my life entirely when I was feeling helpless. It was helping me with my urges to SH and whenever I felt upset I sat down and played more sally face. I told everyone I knew about this game and how it changed my life and talked non-stop about how much I loved it. I still replay the game to this day, and it will forever remain my favorite game of all time. Thank you for making this game, Steve Gabry, you have forever changed and saved my life, and I'll never forget how much your story has impacted me. <3

208670573.review:
A truly beautiful and unique game with an addictive story, fantastic writing, incredible art, and cool music. I can’t believe this was made by a single person! 

The art design reminds me of a 90’s Nikelodeon or MTV cartoon but waaay darker. The narrative takes a well trodden latchkey-kids-caught-up-in-paranormal-stuff template and manages to subvert expectation consistently. I loved the characters (especially Sally), and there is a surprisingly sweet tone, and tons of warmth and love, to go along with the horror and melancholy.

My only criticism is that the actual gameplay, puzzles, and secret-finding are functional at best and actively irritating at worst. Worse still, it is possible to miss VITAL story beats by not completing several very easily missable tasks and obtuse collectibles. I found myself wishing it was more of a walking sim/interactive novel, because when the story really picks up, I don’t want to be exhaustively retreading every location in case I miss an optional puzzle that hides one of the most important cutscenes in the game. It’s just so at odds with the narrative strength of the game. The pacing and the gameplay don’t lend themselves to replays, so I’m sad I have to look up the bits I missed online. 

That said, there is nothing like it and I will be buying the sequel on day 1. I’m so in awe of the dev - what an incredible debut.

208343520.review:
Sally Face has a great premise with great art and great writing, but not enough of each to warrant a full play through with the downsides of the game play and the fall off of the story. The story, for the first two episodes, is extremely interesting and had me hooked. It has the room for thematic depth that was unique and interesting, and had promise to deliver on that potential as the story unraveled. Episode 3 onward ditches the melancholy, meditative concepts in the story for a more thriller, "save the world" narrative that really detracts from the themes and emotional set-ups present in the first two episodes. There was a lot here that could have been used to say something much deeper and explore ideas much more nuanced than just following a narrative plot, and the unique and interesting ways the game play unfolds supports this was the intention, but the writing stops trying to do that, so you are left with a strange game with strange set pieces that really doesn't feel like it is designed to mean anything despite insisting to you that it does.

If you do want to play this game, it is basically required to play with a walkthrough. Most puzzles are a classic adventure game "Find Needed Item" experience and the items in question are just straight hard to find. I explored every level completely and still missed stuff. Sometimes it signals to you that things are interactable, sometimes it doesn't. Sometimes you have to use an item from your inventory, sometimes you just use automatically. It makes the experience of actually traversing the story uninteractably difficult and tedious, which is extra painful since for the first half of the game you are excited to read more of the excellent dialogue and see where the story is going and sometimes you have to stop doing that to find a small item three floor from where you are before you can continue. The game also does its due dilligence to make backtracking (In a game about backtracking) as slow as possible with long room transitions that I can't image are technologically necessary.

It is a unique experience told by someone with clear talent, but had this project cooked in the oven a long while more, I would probably have better things to say about it overall rather than fleeting complements about certain aspects that don't live up to a satisfying whole.

207550974.review:
[b]Sally Face[/b] is a disturbing point-and-click horror adventure game that follows a young boy with a prosthetic face. Players must delve into his tragic past to unravel sinister mysteries and try to make sense of the supernatural events that are occurring. The game consists of 5 episodes released between 2016 and 2019.

It is a brilliant example of a point-and-click adventure, and after nearly a decade since episode one was released, it has garnered something of a cult status. Its dark, often gruesome narrative, depicted in a distinct cartoon-like art style, as well as the moody metal soundtrack, and engaging gameplay, all work together perfectly to create a unique and satisfying experience. The contrast between the game’s most harrowing moments and the more uplifting ones is just part of what makes the experience so impactful. I wholeheartedly enjoyed Sally Face and highly recommend it to fans of the genre or those with a taste for the macabre and supernatural. My only regret is not playing it sooner.

200847575.review:
Sally Face is one of those games that you will remember for a long time even after youve finished it. The storyline, soundtrack, artstyle, characters and basically every aspect of the game clearly had a lot of effort put into it, especially considering that it was all made by one single creator :O Things mentioned above make the game more than enjoyable and absolutely worth playing, particularly if youre into psychological horrors. 

I wouldnt even expect such a simple indie game to be so engaging, yet I ended up spending several hours grinding achievements to max it. The "Escaping Death" one was pure pain and suffering - it forces you to restart the whole episode 5 when you fail even just once, [strike]..I had to redo it like 7 times[/strike]

The plot gets better with each episode and it really keeps you invested. The parts involving the cult are definitely the most fascinating, as well as the most disturbing when you experience another wave of psychological tension in this twisted horror. 200213489.review:
Sally Face – Episode One: Strange Neighbors is a haunting and intriguing introduction to a story-driven indie adventure series that blends psychological horror, mystery, and emotional storytelling into a uniquely stylized experience. Developed almost entirely by solo creator Steve Gabry under the name Portable Moose, the first episode sets the stage for a surreal and deeply personal journey through the eyes of a young boy with a prosthetic face and a traumatic past. While brief in length, this episode establishes a distinctive tone and atmosphere that lingers well beyond its playtime, drawing players into a world of hidden secrets and emotional weight.

The story centers on Sal Fisher, or “Sally Face,” a soft-spoken boy who moves into a new apartment building with his father following a personal tragedy. Sal’s most striking feature is his blue prosthetic mask, which immediately invites curiosity from both characters in the game and players themselves. Rather than playing it for shock value, the mask becomes a symbol of Sal’s inner world—his trauma, isolation, and outsider status. As he explores his new home, it becomes clear that the Addison Apartments are anything but normal. Strange neighbors, whispers of a murder, and a growing sense of unease draw Sal into a mystery that’s far more unsettling than he expected.

Gameplay-wise, Episode One follows a traditional point-and-click structure with light puzzle-solving and exploration. You navigate the apartment building, speak to various residents, collect items, and use clues to unravel the strange events taking place. The gameplay is simple and deliberately paced, allowing the narrative and atmosphere to take center stage. While the puzzles aren’t particularly challenging, they serve the story well and encourage players to pay attention to the dialogue and environments. A few mini-games and interactive sequences break up the pacing, adding variety without overwhelming the experience.

One of the most striking aspects of Sally Face is its art style. The hand-drawn, slightly grotesque character designs and gloomy color palette evoke a feeling of constant discomfort and sadness. There’s a surreal, almost dreamlike quality to the visuals, which fits perfectly with the game’s tone. The design of Sal himself—bright blue hair, stitched mask, and dark eyes—contrasts sharply with the bleakness around him, reinforcing his role as both observer and participant in the story’s strange events. The visuals are complemented by a minimal but effective soundtrack, filled with somber piano notes, unsettling background hums, and eerie silences that amplify tension at just the right moments.

Though short—about an hour or so depending on your pace—Episode One succeeds in establishing a compelling and emotionally complex protagonist and world. It ends on a chilling cliffhanger that hints at deeper psychological and possibly supernatural forces at play, encouraging players to continue through the rest of the episodic series. What it lacks in length, it compensates for with strong writing, memorable characters, and a tone that mixes empathy with dread. The dialogue feels natural and often poignant, particularly in the way it touches on themes like mental health, grief, and alienation without being overly explicit or melodramatic.

That said, the episode’s brevity and minimal gameplay may leave some players wanting more from a traditional game structure. It’s more of a narrative experience than a puzzle-heavy adventure, and its emotional resonance depends heavily on your engagement with Sal as a character. Players looking for action, fast-paced storytelling, or intricate mechanics might find the pace too slow or the interactivity too light. However, for those open to narrative-focused, emotionally driven games, Sally Face offers a compelling and unusually personal experience.

In conclusion, Sally Face – Episode One is a strong and memorable start to a darkly imaginative series. It introduces a mysterious world, a deeply sympathetic protagonist, and a storytelling style that is both eerie and sincere. The combination of unique visuals, unsettling atmosphere, and emotional undercurrents creates an experience that stands out in the indie horror and adventure space. It may be short, but it leaves a lasting impression—and a strong urge to see where Sal’s story goes next.

Rating: 10/10

So now, after years, being able to finally play the game made all these old feelings and worries come back again, leaving a sense of void, but at the same time making me notice how much stronger and happier I’ve become from that time, understanding that there are people that will accept me and love me for who I am, without making me feel different, by just understanding me. 
Playing the game is definitely different than just watching it, and actually completing it is even more different! and difficult! but it’s definitely worth it, so that you can be even more immersed in every detail about the story and the characters.

So I started playing this game because my stepdaughter wanted to play it, and I realized I had purchased it years ago. Let me start by saying this game isn't for children (not that it's marketed like that or anything). Probably 15 or 16 years old at the earliest, depending on their views, knowledge, and feelings about murder, supernatural horror, and trauma. That said, it could be a game to play together, and have conversations about it.

Now as far as a personal review goes, this game is pretty good. If you like point and click puzzles. The puzzles are challenging, and a lot of the objectives are too vague. I found myself backtracking around a lot just clicking on a bunch of things. And I still didn't find all the collectibles.
